title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Lecture 36
text:
If the linear model is appropriate and residuals are roughly normal with constant spread, we can do interval estimation:

Confidence interval for the slope beta1.
Confidence interval for the mean response E(Y | X = x0).
Prediction interval for an individual future Y at x0.

The prediction interval is wider than the mean-response confidence interval because it includes both uncertainty in the mean and individual-to-individual variation.


By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

explain when normal-residual assumptions support interval inference in linear regression;
compute and interpret a confidence interval for slope beta1;
compute and interpret a confidence interval for the mean response at x0;
compute and interpret a prediction interval for an individual response at x0;
explain why prediction intervals are wider than confidence intervals for means;
report test set MSE and test R^2 as primary predictive performance summaries.








Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

library(tidyverse)

set.seed(3501)
n <- 160
exam_dat <- tibble(
  study_hours = runif(n, 1, 15),
  exam_score = 45 + 3.4 * study_hours + rnorm(n, 0, 8)
)






Code
idx_train <- sample(seq_len(nrow(exam_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(exam_dat))
exam_train <- exam_dat[idx_train, ]
exam_test  <- exam_dat[-idx_train, ]

fit_exam <- lm(exam_score ~ study_hours, data = exam_train)
summary(fit_exam)



Call:
lm(formula = exam_score ~ study_hours, data = exam_train)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-16.0920  -4.3112  -0.8295   4.4390  21.3982 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  42.4805     1.3406   31.69   <2e-16 ***
study_hours   3.8436     0.1551   24.78   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 6.664 on 126 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8298,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8284 
F-statistic: 614.2 on 1 and 126 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16







Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(residuals(fit_exam), main = "Residual histogram", xlab = "Residual")
qqnorm(residuals(fit_exam)); qqline(residuals(fit_exam), col = 2)









Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))







Code
confint(fit_exam, "study_hours", level = 0.95)


               2.5 %   97.5 %
study_hours 3.536713 4.150568


Interpretation pattern: we are 95% confident that each additional study hour changes mean exam score by a value in this interval.



For x0 = 10 study hours:


Code
x0 <- tibble(study_hours = 10)
predict(fit_exam, newdata = x0, interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)


       fit      lwr      upr
1 80.91687 79.56421 82.26953


Interpretation: 95% CI for the mean exam score among all students who study 10 hours.





Code
predict(fit_exam, newdata = x0, interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)


       fit      lwr      upr
1 80.91687 67.65902 94.17473


Interpretation: 95% interval for a single future student’s score if they study 10 hours.





Code
mse <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

train_pred <- predict(fit_exam, newdata = exam_train)
test_pred  <- predict(fit_exam, newdata = exam_test)

train_mse <- mse(exam_train$exam_score, train_pred)
test_mse  <- mse(exam_test$exam_score, test_pred)

train_r2 <- 1 - sum((exam_train$exam_score - train_pred)^2) /
  sum((exam_train$exam_score - mean(exam_train$exam_score))^2)
test_r2 <- 1 - sum((exam_test$exam_score - test_pred)^2) /
  sum((exam_test$exam_score - mean(exam_test$exam_score))^2)

tibble(
  set = c("train", "test"),
  MSE = c(train_mse, test_mse),
  R2 = c(train_r2, test_r2)
)


# A tibble: 2 × 3
  set     MSE    R2
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
1 train  43.7 0.830
2 test   66.3 0.767


Use test metrics as your main generalization report:

Test MSE = typical squared prediction error on new cases.
Test R^2 = proportion of test-set variation explained by the linear model.








Code
set.seed(3502)
plant_dat <- tibble(
  dose = runif(95, 0, 12),
  height_cm = 18 + 2.2 * dose + rnorm(95, 0, 5.3)
)

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(plant_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(plant_dat))
plant_train <- plant_dat[idx, ]

fit_plant <- lm(height_cm ~ dose, data = plant_train)
summary(fit_plant)



Call:
lm(formula = height_cm ~ dose, data = plant_train)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-10.7136  -3.3558   0.5898   3.7855   8.9421 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  18.3691     1.3412   13.70   <2e-16 ***
dose          2.1394     0.1706   12.54   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 4.961 on 74 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:   0.68, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6756 
F-statistic: 157.2 on 1 and 74 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


Code
# CI for slope beta1
confint(fit_plant, "dose")


        2.5 %   97.5 %
dose 1.799457 2.479396


Code
# CI for mean response at x0 = 7
new_x <- tibble(dose = 7)
predict(fit_plant, newdata = new_x, interval = "confidence")


       fit     lwr     upr
1 33.34505 32.2105 34.4796


Code
# Prediction interval for an individual at x0 = 7
predict(fit_plant, newdata = new_x, interval = "prediction")


       fit     lwr     upr
1 33.34505 23.3956 43.2945








Code
set.seed(3503)
heart_dat <- tibble(
  exercise_min = runif(110, 0, 60),
  resting_hr = 79 - 0.30 * exercise_min + rnorm(110, 0, 4.8)
)

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(heart_dat)), size = 0.8 * nrow(heart_dat))
heart_train <- heart_dat[idx, ]

fit_heart <- lm(resting_hr ~ exercise_min, data = heart_train)
summary(fit_heart)



Call:
lm(formula = resting_hr ~ exercise_min, data = heart_train)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-11.6123  -3.1631   0.3452   2.9383  14.2460 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  80.00362    1.17719  67.962  < 2e-16 ***
exercise_min -0.31807    0.03237  -9.827 1.02e-15 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 4.854 on 86 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5289,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5235 
F-statistic: 96.57 on 1 and 86 DF,  p-value: 1.023e-15


Code
# CI for slope beta1
confint(fit_heart, "exercise_min")


                  2.5 %     97.5 %
exercise_min -0.3824177 -0.2537277


Code
# CI for mean resting HR at x0 = 30 min
new_x <- tibble(exercise_min = 30)
predict(fit_heart, newdata = new_x, interval = "confidence")


       fit      lwr      upr
1 70.46144 69.41853 71.50435


Code
# Prediction interval for one person at x0 = 30 min
predict(fit_heart, newdata = new_x, interval = "prediction")


       fit      lwr      upr
1 70.46144 60.75523 80.16766







Slope CI answers: what plausible values for the linear effect beta1 are supported by data?
Mean-response CI answers: where is the average outcome for all units at x0?
Prediction interval answers: where might one future individual outcome fall at x0?
Keep reporting test MSE and test R^2 for predictive performance on new data.

title: Lecture 36
Lecture 36
text:
Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

library(tidyverse)

set.seed(3501)
n <- 160
exam_dat <- tibble(
  study_hours = runif(n, 1, 15),
  exam_score = 45 + 3.4 * study_hours + rnorm(n, 0, 8)

Call:

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-16.0920  -4.3112  -0.8295   4.4390  21.3982 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  42.4805     1.3406   31.69   <2e-16 ***
study_hours   3.8436     0.1551   24.78   <2e-16 ***
---

Multiple R-squared:  0.8298,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8284

hist(residuals(fit_exam), main = "Residual histogram", xlab = "Residual")
qqnorm(residuals(fit_exam)); qqline(residuals(fit_exam), col = 2)









Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))







Code
confint(fit_exam, "study_hours", level = 0.95)


               2.5 %   97.5 %
study_hours 3.536713 4.150568


Interpretation pattern: we are 95% confident that each additional study hour changes mean exam score by a value in this interval.



For x0 = 10 study hours:

Interpretation: 95% CI for the mean exam score among all students who study 10 hours.





Code

       fit      lwr      upr
1 80.91687 67.65902 94.17473


Interpretation: 95% interval for a single future student’s score if they study 10 hours.





Code
mse <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

train_pred <- predict(fit_exam, newdata = exam_train)
test_pred  <- predict(fit_exam, newdata = exam_test)

train_mse <- mse(exam_train$exam_score, train_pred)
test_mse  <- mse(exam_test$exam_score, test_pred)

train_r2 <- 1 - sum((exam_train$exam_score - train_pred)^2) /
  sum((exam_train$exam_score - mean(exam_train$exam_score))^2)

  sum((exam_test$exam_score - mean(exam_test$exam_score))^2)

tibble(
  set = c("train", "test"),
  MSE = c(train_mse, test_mse),
  R2 = c(train_r2, test_r2)

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  set     MSE    R2
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
1 train  43.7 0.830
2 test   66.3 0.767


Use test metrics as your main generalization report:

Test MSE = typical squared prediction error on new cases.
Test R^2 = proportion of test-set variation explained by the linear model.

title: Lecture 36
text:
Slope CI answers: what plausible values for the linear effect beta1 are supported by data?
Mean-response CI answers: where is the average outcome for all units at x0?
Prediction interval answers: where might one future individual outcome fall at x0?
Keep reporting test MSE and test R^2 for predictive performance on new data.
